parts.name: Opening
what_to_do: Gather the group in a circle or huddle; eyes at your level
Settle them with three slow breaths together
Say: 'Today we start something new. Not physical training—mental training.'
parts.what_to_say: You train your legs, your arms, your moves. But what about your mind? That's what we're doing today.

parts.name: Core concept
what_to_do: Use your own body as the demo: point to legs, arms, then head
Ask one player: 'What do you do when you feel scared before a game?' (listen, validate)
Introduce the core idea simply: 'Mental fitness is like physical fitness. You can get better at it.'
parts.what_to_say: You're not bad at this yet. You just haven't learned it yet. And when you start young, you get really good at it.

parts.name: On-pitch drill
what_to_do: Drill: 'Feelings Spot' — each player picks a corner or zone of the pitch
Name an emotion (excited, scared, angry, proud). Players physically move to that zone if they've felt it
Repeat with 4–5 emotions. Keep tempo light and playful
Bring back together: 'These feelings are normal. Everyone has them. You're going to learn what to do with them.'
parts.what_to_say: When you feel scared, your body is trying to help you. It's not bad—it's your body getting ready.

parts.name: Close
what_to_do: Sit in a line or circle; ask 2–3 players: 'What's one thing you felt in this session?'
Affirm their answer without judgment
Close with the remember line
parts.what_to_say: My mind is a skill I can train.

guides.age_band_notes: Keep language concrete. Avoid abstract psychology. Use their body and physical space to teach the concept.

parts.name: Opening
what_to_do: Gather the group seated or standing comfortably
Acknowledge them: 'You train your technique every session. Today we're training something else.'
Play the video (approx 90–100 sec) with full audio
parts.what_to_say: Every athlete from grassroots to professional has moments where their mind gets in the way. That's not weakness—it's a skill you haven't been trained in yet.

parts.name: Core concept
what_to_do: After video, draw the comparison on a board or in the sand: physical fitness vs. mental fitness
Ask: 'What does physical fitness training look like?' (repeats, consistency, progression)
Point out: 'Mental fitness is identical. Same training principles. Same results.'
Use one relatable moment: 'Nerves before a big game, or losing trust after a mistake.'
parts.what_to_say: You can build mental fitness the same way you build physical fitness: through consistent, structured practice.

parts.name: On-pitch drill
what_to_do: Drill: 'Emotion Scan' — players stand in a circle or spread across pitch
Call out a sport moment: 'You just missed a penalty.' Players physically show how their body felt (shoulders, face, stance)
Repeat with 3–4 scenarios (good pass, tough loss, nervous before match)
Debrief: 'Notice: everyone's body changes with different moments. That's normal. You learn to notice it and work with it.'
parts.what_to_say: Mental fitness isn't a personality trait. It's a skill. And like any skill, you get better through practice.

parts.name: Close
what_to_do: Ask 2–3 players: 'What's one moment this week where you noticed your mind helping or hindering?'
Affirm and thank them for noticing
End with the remember line
parts.what_to_say: My mind is a skill I can train.

guides.age_band_notes: This age band can handle the video without over-explaining. Trust the content; add only clarification if players ask. Emphasise the science angle lightly: 'Researchers have found this works.'

parts.name: Opening
what_to_do: Brief contextual frame: 'This isn't coaching psychology. This is performance training. Elite athletes train their minds like they train their bodies.'
Play the video (approx 110 sec) with full audio
Note the research reference in the video for credibility
parts.what_to_say: Research shows athletes who train mental skills early gain a measurable edge. That's not opinion—that's evidence.

parts.name: Core concept
what_to_do: Debrief the video: 'You heard the word eustress. That's good stress—pressure that sharpens you, not crushes you.'
Introduce the foundation: 'Mental fitness = awareness + control. You notice what's happening. You choose what to do with it.'
Give one relatable technical example: 'When you miss a pass, do you spiral into self-criticism (loss of focus), or extract the learning and reset (mental fitness)?'
Frame it competitively: 'The players who process this fastest have an edge in the final 20 minutes of tight matches.'
parts.what_to_say: Elite performers don't eliminate doubt. They process it faster. That's a trainable system.

parts.name: On-pitch drill
what_to_do: Drill: 'Moment Audit' — give a realistic match scenario (last-minute equaliser conceded, poor personal performance, dropped for next match)
Players write or discuss: what's your first mental response? (judgment, fear, motivation?)
Reframe each response: 'That's your baseline. Today we start training a faster recovery.'
Quick live drill: 'Next three rondos, after every loss of possession, show me a reset (breath, reset word, physical shake-off).'
Observe and reinforce the reset behaviours when you see them
parts.what_to_say: Mental fitness training is like technical training. You notice the gap. You work the gap. You close the gap.

parts.name: Close
what_to_do: Ask: 'What's one moment this week where you want to process faster or reset cleaner?'
Optional: 'This is your baseline. In 12 weeks, that recovery time is measurably shorter.'
End with the remember line
parts.what_to_say: My mind is a skill I can train.

guides.age_band_notes: This cohort responds well to competitive framing and measurement. Mention 'measurable improvement' and 'elite athletes do this.' Avoid over-explaining; trust their cognitive capacity.
equipment_needed: Optional: whiteboard or paper for moment audit

parts.name: Opening
what_to_do: Ask: 'In your last game, what did you feel?' (Happy? Scared? Angry?)
Listen to 1–2 answers; validate: 'That's real.'
Introduce: 'Those feelings aren't problems. They're messages from your body.'
parts.what_to_say: When you're scared, your body is trying to help. When you're angry, there's energy there.

parts.name: Core concept
what_to_do: Use hand gestures or simple drawings to show: fear = alert, anger = energy, happiness = connection
Say: 'The trick isn't to stop feeling things. It's to understand what your feelings are telling you.'
Model it yourself: 'Before a big game, I feel nervous. My body is saying: this matters. I'm ready.'
parts.what_to_say: You start learning to notice your feelings like a detective. Not as a problem. As a clue.

parts.name: On-pitch drill
what_to_do: Drill: 'Feeling Detective' — call out moments; players show their body response
Moments: 'You score a goal' (celebrate), 'You miss an easy pass' (frustrated), 'You get fouled' (angry), 'You're about to play' (nervous)
After each, ask: 'What is that feeling telling your body to do?' (Celebrate connection, Try harder, Stay alert, Get ready)
Wrap: 'When you notice what you're feeling, you can decide what to do next.'
parts.what_to_say: When you notice what you feel, you're in control.

parts.name: Close
what_to_do: Ask one player: 'What's one feeling you had today? What was it telling you?'
Affirm their answer
Repeat the remember line with them
parts.what_to_say: I notice what I feel—and I decide what I do with it.

guides.safeguarding_note: If a player shares difficult emotions (grief, family stress), listen with care but don't probe. Affirm and say: 'That's real. You can talk to your parent/coach about that too.' Keep the focus on noticing, not fixing.
guides.age_band_notes: Emotions are concrete at this age. Use body language, facial expressions, and simple language (angry, sad, excited, scared). Avoid clinical terms.

parts.name: Opening
what_to_do: Frame: 'Today's about understanding what your body is telling you.'
Play video (approx 105 sec)
Pause after key line: 'The problem isn't having emotions. The problem is not knowing what to do with them.'
parts.what_to_say: Your emotions aren't in charge. You are. That's a skill, and it starts now.

parts.name: Core concept
what_to_do: Debrief: 'You heard—frustration means you care, nerves mean it matters, anger can be energy if you know how to use it.'
Draw a simple chart on a board: Emotion → Signal → What You Can Do
Fill in one example: Frustration → I care about this → Channel it into effort
Ask players for one more example (e.g. nerves → importance → use the activation)
parts.what_to_say: When you understand what you're feeling, you can decide what to do with it instead of just reacting.

parts.name: On-pitch drill
what_to_do: Drill: 'Emotion-to-Action' — 1v1 or 2v2 small-sided game
Mid-drill, pause and ask: 'Who just felt frustrated [or nervous, or excited]? What did you do with that feeling?'
Affirm the player who channelled it productively (worked harder, stayed calm, used the energy)
Repeat 2–3 times: pause, identify emotion, name the choice they made
parts.what_to_say: Notice what you're feeling in the moment. Then decide what to do with it.

parts.name: Close
what_to_do: Ask: 'What's one emotion you felt today? What did it tell you?'
Affirm and close
parts.what_to_say: I notice what I feel—and I decide what I do with it.

guides.safeguarding_note: If a player discloses sustained anxiety, anger management issues, or emotional distress, listen supportively and refer to the club welfare officer. Note: 'It sounds like this is important for you. Let's make sure your coach/parents know so they can help.'
guides.age_band_notes: This age band begins to understand the nuance that emotions carry information. Avoid labelling feelings as 'good' or 'bad'—reframe as 'useful' or 'less useful right now.'

parts.name: Opening
what_to_do: Open with: 'Today is about emotional granularity—learning to distinguish types of nerves, types of focus, types of drive.'
Play video (approx 115 sec); let the neuroscience language land
parts.what_to_say: Elite athletes don't eliminate emotions. They develop emotional granularity—precision in understanding what they're feeling and why.

parts.name: Core concept
what_to_do: Debrief the concept: 'Emotional granularity means you move beyond vague labels.'
Demo: 'Not just 'nervous'—but anticipatory nerves (before the match), performance nerves (during), or recovery nerves (after). Each needs a different response.'
Ask: 'Give me two types of focus you've experienced—maybe hyper-focus on the ball, or broad awareness of the shape. That's granularity.'
Link to performance: 'The better your emotional literacy, the faster you can choose a response that actually helps.'
parts.what_to_say: Emotional granularity is the foundation of regulation. Precision in naming your state is the first step to managing it.

parts.name: On-pitch drill
what_to_do: Drill: 'State Naming Under Pressure' — 1v1 intense small-sided game with rotation
After 90 seconds, pause each pair: 'Name your state right now. Not just 'tired'—what's your energy like? Focused or scattered? Driven or hesitant?'
Player names their state; partner reflects it back: 'So you're energised but scattered?'
Resume; repeat once more. The naming itself sharpens their awareness
parts.what_to_say: Precision in naming your emotional state is a performance skill. When you name it accurately, you can respond accurately.

parts.name: Close
what_to_do: Debrief: 'How did it feel to name your state in the moment? Did it change how you played?'
Optional: 'This is what elite athletes call meta-awareness—you're watching yourself from outside.'
Close with the remember line
parts.what_to_say: I notice what I feel—and I decide what I do with it.

guides.safeguarding_note: If a player expresses sustained emotional difficulty (depression, anxiety, anger management), affirm and refer: 'This sounds like something worth exploring with a professional. I can help connect you with the club welfare officer.'
guides.age_band_notes: This cohort can handle psychological terminology ('meta-awareness,' 'emotional granularity') if paired with practical context. Emphasise the competitive advantage of precision.

parts.name: Opening
what_to_do: Ask: 'Can you build confidence like you build strength?'
Wait for answers; affirm any that point to practice or repetition
Introduce: 'Yes. Confidence comes from three things. Let me show you.'
parts.what_to_say: Confidence isn't something you're born with. It's something you build.

parts.name: Core concept
what_to_do: Draw or use your hands to show three 'pillars' (or 'legs' of a triangle):
1. Stuff you've already done well (past success)
2. Actual skills you have (current capability)
3. What you say to yourself (self-talk)
Demo with yourself: 'I've trained hard this week. I know I can pass well. And I tell myself: I've got this.'
Emphasise: 'When one of these is low, you still have the other two. You can always rebuild.'
parts.what_to_say: Even on a bad day, you can rebuild your confidence because you have real evidence underneath it.

parts.name: On-pitch drill
what_to_do: Drill: 'Confidence Build' — small-sided game with breaks
After 3 minutes, pause and ask one player: 'What have you done well in this game?' (Listen, affirm)
Ask another: 'What's a skill you know you have?' (Affirm)
Ask a third: 'What do you tell yourself when you need to be confident?' (Affirm)
Resume play; repeat after another 3 minutes with different players
parts.what_to_say: Your confidence is real. It's based on things you've actually done and things you actually can do.

parts.name: Close
what_to_do: Ask one player: 'What's one thing you've done well today?'
Affirm and close with the remember line
parts.what_to_say: My confidence is built on evidence. I can always rebuild it.

guides.age_band_notes: Young players often struggle with self-belief. If a player says 'I'm not good at anything,' gently redirect: 'Let me point something out: you showed up today. You tried. That's evidence.' Build it brick by brick.

parts.name: Opening
what_to_do: Frame: 'Confidence isn't something you have or don't have. It's something you can build—exactly like fitness.'
Play video (approx 115 sec)
Pause after: 'Confidence doesn't disappear. It just needs rebuilding.'
parts.what_to_say: Think about building fitness. You train, you repeat, you improve. Confidence works the same way.

parts.name: Core concept
what_to_do: Draw the three sides of the confidence triangle on a board or in the sand:
1. What you've already done (evidence bank)
2. What you can actually do (real skills)
3. What you say to yourself (self-talk)
Demo: 'Even after a bad game, I still have evidence of good training. I still have real skills. And I can still tell myself something that helps.'
Key point: 'Elite athletes fill their evidence bank deliberately. They remember what they've done. They know what they can do.'
parts.what_to_say: You're going to start filling your confidence bank with actual evidence—not empty words, but real proof.

parts.name: On-pitch drill
what_to_do: Drill: 'Confidence Bank Builder' — 1v1 or 2v2 game with structured pauses
Every 2 minutes, pause one player and ask: 'What's one thing you've done well in this game?' (Document mentally or write it down)
Later ask: 'What's one skill you know you have?' (Affirm)
Later: 'What do you tell yourself when you need confidence?' (Affirm)
Reinforce: 'That's your confidence triangle. All three things are real.'
parts.what_to_say: Your evidence bank is real. The more you notice it, the more available it is when you need it.

parts.name: Close
what_to_do: Ask 1–2 players: 'What's your one piece of evidence today?'
Affirm; close with the remember line
parts.what_to_say: My confidence is built on evidence. I can always rebuild it.

guides.safeguarding_note: If a player struggles to identify any success, help them see small wins: 'You tried. You stayed engaged. You asked for feedback. Those are pieces of evidence.'
guides.age_band_notes: This age band sometimes dismisses their own competence. Frame it competitively: 'Elite athletes keep a running list of what they're good at. That's what we're doing.'
equipment_needed: Whiteboard/marker or paper to document evidence

parts.name: Opening
what_to_do: Frame: 'Bandura's research on self-efficacy shows confidence is built from four sources—and we're starting with three.'
Play video (approx 120 sec); emphasise the research grounding
parts.what_to_say: Confidence isn't a fixed trait. It's a trainable construct built from mastery experiences, actual skills, and self-talk.

parts.name: Core concept
what_to_do: Debrief the four sources (mastery, skill, self-talk, observational learning): 'You've got all four available right now in your team.'
Draw the triangle with all three sides: mastery (what you've accomplished), skill (what you can do), self-talk (what you tell yourself)
Key insight: 'Even when one source is depleted—after a poor game—you have access to the other two. Resilience is about using all three.'
Competitive angle: 'Elite athletes don't lose confidence. They reconstruct it deliberately by accessing all three sources.'
parts.what_to_say: Your evidence bank is bigger than you think. Mastery experiences, technical skills, and your own internal narrative—all of it counts.

parts.name: On-pitch drill
what_to_do: Drill: 'Evidence-Based Reconstruction'—1v1 or 2v2 with deliberate pauses after mistakes
After a player makes an error: ask immediately, 'One mastery experience this season,' then 'One technical skill you've developed,' then 'One thing you'd tell yourself right now.'
The naming itself is the reset; then resume play
Repeat 2–3 times; reinforce: 'You just reconstructed your confidence from all three sources. That's a skill.'
parts.what_to_say: Confidence reconstruction isn't about feeling better. It's about accessing evidence that's always been there.

parts.name: Close
what_to_do: Debrief: 'Which source—mastery, skill, or self-talk—was most accessible to you today?'
Affirm and explain: 'That's your go-to. The others are backups. But they're all real.'
parts.what_to_say: My confidence is built on evidence. I can always rebuild it.

guides.age_band_notes: This cohort can handle the research framing and will respond to competitive angle: 'Players who use all three sources recover faster from setbacks.'

parts.name: Opening
what_to_do: Ask: 'What do you do after you make a mistake?'
Listen to 1–2 answers without judgment
Introduce: 'Today you're going to learn a different way.'
parts.what_to_say: Your mistake is telling you something. It's like a message from your body about what to work on next.

parts.name: Core concept
what_to_do: Use an example: 'Your foot was in the wrong spot. You passed too soft. You weren't ready for that move.'
Say: 'That's not bad. That's useful. Now you know what to work on.'
Model: 'The best players in the world make mistakes all the time. They just learn from them fast.'
parts.what_to_say: Notice what went wrong. Learn what to do better. Take a breath. Do it again. That's how you get stronger.

parts.name: On-pitch drill
what_to_do: Drill: 'Notice-Learn-Move' — small-sided game with coached pauses
When a player makes a mistake, stop and ask: 'What happened?' (Notice) → 'What does that tell you?' (Learn) → 'Okay, let's go again' (Move)
Keep it positive and quick; don't dwell
Repeat 3–4 times throughout the session; reinforce the pattern
parts.what_to_say: When you mess up, you're not upset—you're just learning.

parts.name: Close
what_to_do: Ask: 'Did anyone notice what they did wrong and what to work on?'
Affirm; close with the remember line
parts.what_to_say: My mistake is just telling me what to practice.

guides.age_band_notes: At this age, mistakes can trigger shame. Normalise them intensely: 'Every player here made a mistake today. And you're all learning. That's the whole thing.'

parts.name: Opening
what_to_do: Frame: 'What you do in the second after a mistake changes everything.'
Play video (approx 115 sec); let the key line land: 'While you're beating yourself up, the game has moved on.'
parts.what_to_say: Most athletes spiral. Elite athletes process the mistake and move on. That's a trainable skill.

parts.name: Core concept
what_to_do: Introduce the reset sequence: Notice → Learn → Reset → Move On
Draw or write it clearly; point out: 'The whole thing takes 5 seconds.'
Key insight: 'Mistakes aren't failures. They're data. The best athletes take more risks because they process mistakes faster.'
parts.what_to_say: Notice what happened. What does it tell you? Let it go. Back in the game. Four steps, five seconds. That's the skill.

parts.name: On-pitch drill
what_to_do: Drill: 'Reset Protocol'—1v1 or 2v2 with coached resets
When a player makes an error, pause and say: 'Reset. Notice. Learn. Go.'
The player names the mistake, the learning, and shakes it off (physical gesture)
Resume immediately
Repeat 3–4 times per player; make it rhythmic
parts.what_to_say: The reset is a physical and mental skill. You practice it until it's automatic.

parts.name: Close
what_to_do: Ask: 'Who felt the reset working? Did it help you stay in the game?'
Affirm; close with the remember line
parts.what_to_say: I notice, learn, reset, and move on. Every time.

guides.age_band_notes: Some players internalise mistakes as personal failures. Emphasise repeatedly: 'Every mistake is just information. It's not about you as a person. It's about what you do next.'

parts.name: Opening
what_to_do: Frame: 'Elite athletes process errors in real time. Rumination is expensive—it costs you focus on the next play.'
Play video (approx 120 sec); let the research on error processing land
parts.what_to_say: Research on error processing in sport is clear: the faster you extract the learning, the faster you reset.

parts.name: Core concept
what_to_do: Debrief the error-processing cycle: Notice → Name → Learn → Reset
Key: 'Naming the variable matters. Not vague self-blame, but specific observation.'
Example: 'Not 'I messed up,' but 'My positioning was 30cm off. Next time I adjust left.'
Competitive framing: 'The best players in your league take more risks than their peers because they have the fastest error-recovery loop.'
parts.what_to_say: Your mistake is just a data point. Your response to it is what separates elite from average.

parts.name: On-pitch drill
what_to_do: Drill: 'Variable Naming'—1v1 or 2v2 game with deliberate error pauses
When an error occurs, pause and ask: 'Which variable? Positioning, timing, or decision?'
Player names it; you affirm the precision
Player names the correction; resume
Repeat until the naming becomes automatic mid-game
parts.what_to_say: Specific error naming is a trainable skill. The more precise you are, the faster you correct.

parts.name: Close
what_to_do: Debrief: 'How did it feel naming the variable instead of just spiralling?'
Affirm; optional: 'That's your competitive edge—fastest error processing in the match.'
parts.what_to_say: My mistakes are data. My response is what separates elite from average.

guides.age_band_notes: This cohort can handle neuroscience language and will respond to 'this is how elite athletes think.' Emphasise the measurable difference in performance.

parts.name: Opening
what_to_do: Ask: 'You know that feeling when a game feels easy and you're just playing?'
Wait for recognition (nods or 'yes')
Introduce: 'That's focus. And you can learn to get it back whenever you want.'
parts.what_to_say: Focus is like a light. You point it where you want to look. Some times you shine it on one thing. Other times you see everything.

parts.name: Core concept
what_to_do: Demo with your own body: point your head where you're focusing, then shift your focus
Say: 'Your brain gets lots of noise—your coach, the crowd, what you had for lunch. Good players turn off the noise by pointing their focus where it matters.'
Key: 'Your focus is like a muscle. The more you practice pointing it, the faster it goes where you want.'
parts.what_to_say: Everyone's focus drifts. The trick is getting it back fast.

parts.name: On-pitch drill
what_to_do: Drill: 'Focus Lock'—small-sided game with a focus word (e.g., 'Ball' or 'Next Pass')
When a player's focus drifts (you notice distraction), say their focus word loudly
They lock back in and resume
Repeat throughout the drill; make it rhythmic and positive
Optional: each player picks their own focus word
parts.what_to_say: When your mind wanders, just say your focus word. Then lock back in. You're training your focus every time you do that.

parts.name: Close
what_to_do: Ask: 'Did you notice your focus drifting? Did you get it back?'
Affirm; close with the remember line
parts.what_to_say: I can always bring my focus back. I'm getting better at it.

guides.age_band_notes: Young players have naturally short attention spans. Frame it positively: 'Your brain is supposed to wander. Everyone's does. We're just practicing getting it back faster.'

parts.name: Opening
what_to_do: Frame: 'Focus is a skill. And like every skill, it has to be trained.'
Play video (approx 120 sec); let the neuroscience of attention land
parts.what_to_say: Elite athletes don't have fewer distractions. They've trained their attention to come back faster when it drifts.

parts.name: Core concept
what_to_do: Introduce the two types of focus: Narrow (locked on one thing like the ball) and Broad (whole picture)
Say: 'Great players switch between both automatically. That's the skill.'
Key insight: 'You're not trying to stop your attention drifting. It will drift. You're training faster recovery.'
Demo: 'Watch the ball. Now notice the whole field. Now back to the ball. That's the cycle.'
parts.what_to_say: Focus is trainable. The gap between drift and return is where the work happens.

parts.name: On-pitch drill
what_to_do: Drill: 'Narrow-Broad-Return'—1v1 or 2v2 game with focus switches
Call out: 'Narrow!' (players lock on ball)
Call out: 'Broad!' (players see whole field and shape)
Resume play; repeat every 30 seconds
Observe: who switches fastest? Affirm that focus flexibility
parts.what_to_say: The best players compress this cycle. They return to task focus in milliseconds, not seconds.

parts.name: Close
what_to_do: Ask: 'When did your focus feel locked in today?'
Affirm; close with the remember line
parts.what_to_say: I don't have to be perfect. I just have to keep coming back.

guides.age_band_notes: This age band often thinks perfect focus means no distraction. Reframe: 'Even the best players' attention shifts. The skill is the recovery, not the prevention.'

parts.name: Opening
what_to_do: Frame: 'Elite focus isn't sustained attention. Your brain can't do that. Elite focus is attentional flexibility—the ability to switch and recover fast.'
Play video (approx 120 sec); the neuroscience language will resonate
parts.what_to_say: Research on expert athletes shows the narrowest drift-to-return cycles correlate directly with performance consistency.

parts.name: Core concept
what_to_do: Debrief the attentional cycle: Narrow → Broad → Drift → Return
Key: 'Drift is neurological, not a failure. Return speed is trainable.'
Competitive angle: 'The players with the tightest drift-return cycles in your league are the ones playing in the final 20 minutes. And that's why.'
Demo: 'Watch the ball (narrow). Check the field (broad). Notice the distraction (drift). Snap back (return). That's one cycle. Compress it. Master it.'
parts.what_to_say: Your competitive edge is built on attentional fitness. Drift-return speed is measurable. And it's trainable.

parts.name: On-pitch drill
what_to_do: Drill: 'Compression Cycles'—1v1 or 2v2 game with focus timer
Every 45 seconds, pause and ask: 'How many times did your attention drift and return in that window?'
Next cycle, push for faster return: 'Tighter cycles.'
Repeat; reinforce the measurement: 'The faster your cycle, the more consistent you are under pressure.'
parts.what_to_say: Make that cycle automatic. When you're in match conditions under pressure, that trained cycle becomes your edge.

parts.name: Close
what_to_do: Debrief: 'How did it feel focusing on tightening your cycle instead of just
Affirm; optional: 'You're building attentional fitness. That's elite-level mental training.'
parts.what_to_say: My focus isn't fixed. It's flexible. And my recovery speed is my competitive edge.

guides.age_band_notes: This cohort will respond well to the neuroscience framing and measurable component. Emphasise: 'This is how elite players train their attention.'

parts.name: Opening
what_to_do: Ask: 'Before a big game, what happens to your body?'
Listen to 1–2 answers (heart fast, hands shake, tight chest)
Say: 'That's nerves. And everyone gets them. But here's the thing: your breath can change how your body feels.'
parts.what_to_say: You can't tell your heart to slow down. You can tell your breathing to slow down. And when you do, your heart listens.

parts.name: Core concept
what_to_do: Teach box breathing with your own body as demo: 'Breathe in slow for four counts. Hold for three or four. Breathe out slow for four. Hold for three.'
Demo it with them; take them through one or two cycles together
Say: 'Do this before you play and you feel different. Your body can feel the difference.'
parts.what_to_say: Your breath is the fastest way to tell your body you're okay. It always works.

parts.name: On-pitch drill
what_to_do: Drill: 'Breath and Play'—small-sided game with breathing pauses
Every 2 minutes, stop and take one box-breathing cycle together
Resume play; repeat 3–4 times
Optional: ask if they notice the difference after breathing
parts.what_to_say: When you're nervous before you play, this is what you do. Slow breath in, hold, slow breath out, hold.

parts.name: Close
what_to_do: Ask: 'Did anyone feel the difference when we did the slow breathing?'
Affirm; close with the remember line
parts.what_to_say: My breath is my calm button. I can use it whenever I need to.

guides.age_band_notes: Young children respond well to concrete, physical practices. Box breathing works immediately for this age group. Repeat it multiple times.

parts.name: Opening
what_to_do: Frame: 'Your body and mind are connected. When you're stressed, your brain reads that as danger.'
Play video (approx 115 sec); let the neuroscience of vagal regulation land
parts.what_to_say: Controlled breathing sends a direct signal to your brain: we're safe, we're ready, we're in control.

parts.name: Core concept
what_to_do: Teach box breathing live: in for 4, hold for 4, out for 4, hold for 4
Take them through two full cycles together; make it rhythmic
Say: 'Box breathing is used by special forces and elite athletes. It takes under two minutes and it always works.'
Key: 'You can't always control your thoughts. But you can control your breathing. And when you do, your thoughts follow.'
parts.what_to_say: Your breath is the fastest, most reliable reset you have. It's always available. And it always works.

parts.name: On-pitch drill
what_to_do: Drill: 'Pressure-and-Reset'—1v1 or 2v2 game with coached resets
Mid-game, introduce a stressor (e.g., 'Winner stays on; loser sits')
If you notice a player showing stress (tight shoulders, rushed play), say: 'Reset.'
Player stops, takes one box-breathing cycle, resumes
Repeat 3–4 times; reinforce: 'When things get hard, you breathe. Then you play.'
parts.what_to_say: Do it once before a drill. Do it after a stressful moment on the pitch. Do it in the changing room when nerves kick in.

parts.name: Close
what_to_do: Ask: 'Did anyone feel the reset working when things got intense?'
Affirm; close with the remember line
parts.what_to_say: My breath is always available. It always works.

guides.age_band_notes: This age band is receptive to the mind-body connection. Emphasise the science: 'Your nervous system actually changes when you breathe slowly. It's real biology.'

parts.name: Opening
what_to_do: Frame: 'Your nervous system operates on an axis: sympathetic and parasympathetic. Controlled breathing activates parasympathetic recovery.'
Play video (approx 120 sec); let the vagal nerve and eustress language land
parts.what_to_say: One 60-second cycle of box breathing shifts your nervous system state measurably. That's neurology, not metaphor.

parts.name: Core concept
what_to_do: Debrief the nervous system axis: sympathetic (fight-or-flight) vs. parasympathetic (recovery)
Key: 'Too much sympathetic dominance triggers risk aversion. You need parasympathetic tone to regulate.'
Teach box breathing: in for 4, hold for 4, out for 4, hold for 4
Take them through one cycle; point out: 'Notice the shift in your physical state. That's measurable.'
Competitive frame: 'Players who master nervous system regulation under pressure perform measurably better in the final stages of matches.'
parts.what_to_say: Your nervous system is trainable. Your breath is your regulation tool.

parts.name: On-pitch drill
what_to_do: Drill: 'Regulation Under Pressure'—1v1 or 2v2 game with escalating intensity
Introduce high-stakes scenarios (tournament rules, elimination)
When you notice sympathetic dominance (rushed play, poor decision-making), intervene: 'Regulation cycle.'
Player takes one box-breathing protocol; resumes with measurably sharper decision-making
Repeat; reinforce: 'You're not managing anxiety. You're channelling eustress into performance.'
parts.what_to_say: Before competition, before stressful moments, after errors—box breathing is your physiological toolkit.

parts.name: Close
what_to_do: Debrief: 'How did your decision-making shift after the breathing intervention?'
Affirm; optional: 'That's your parasympathetic brake in action. Master it and you own your activation state.'
parts.what_to_say: My nervous system is trainable. My breath is my regulation tool.

guides.age_band_notes: This cohort will respond powerfully to neuroscience language. Emphasise the measurable, biological aspects and the performance advantage.

parts.name: Opening
what_to_do: Ask: 'You warm up your legs, your arms, your skills. But do you warm up your mind?'
Wait for recognition
Say: 'The best athletes do. They run through a quick routine before every game.'
parts.what_to_say: It takes three minutes. Maybe five. And it helps your body and brain get ready together.

parts.name: Core concept
what_to_do: Introduce the four-step routine: Breathe → Say your cue word → Picture yourself → Set your goal
Demo each step with yourself
Say: 'When you do it the same way every time, your body learns. Your brain learns. And game time feels familiar. Safe. Ready.'
parts.what_to_say: Same routine, same game. Ready, set, go.

parts.name: On-pitch drill
what_to_do: Drill: 'Design Your Routine'—have each player create their own mental warm-up
Walk them through: pick your cue word (e.g., 'Go,' 'Ready,' 'Strong')
Visualise: ask them to close their eyes and see themselves playing well
Set intention: 'What do you want to bring today?'
Have them run through it once before a small-sided game; repeat it again later
Observe and affirm when you see them doing it
parts.what_to_say: Keep it short. Keep it the same. Use it before every game.

parts.name: Close
what_to_do: Ask: 'Who designed their routine? Will you use it before your next game?'
Affirm; close with the remember line
parts.what_to_say: Same routine, same game. Ready, set, go.

guides.age_band_notes: Young players often have superstitions. Frame this as more deliberate than that: 'It's not luck. It's training your brain to be ready.'

parts.name: Opening
what_to_do: Frame: 'Consistent athletes have consistent routines. Not superstitions—deliberate sequences.'
Play video (approx 115 sec); emphasise: 'When you repeat it consistently, it becomes an anchor.'
parts.what_to_say: Your body and mind learn: when I run this sequence—it's game time.

parts.name: Core concept
what_to_do: Introduce the four components: Breathing (settle nerves) → Cue word (switch on) → Visualisation (see yourself) → Intention (what you want to bring)
Demo each one; take them through a live example
Say: 'Make it yours. Keep it simple. Use it every single time. That's how an anchor works.'
parts.what_to_say: When you repeat it consistently, it becomes an anchor. Your body and mind learn: it's game time.

parts.name: On-pitch drill
what_to_do: Drill: 'Routine Design and Execution'—each player creates and practises their routine
Have them fill in: breathing (how many?), cue word (what word?), visualisation (what do you see?), intention (what do you bring?)
Have them run through it once before a game; observe and affirm
Have them run through it again before a different activity; reinforce: 'Same sequence = same readiness.'
parts.what_to_say: Make it yours. Keep it simple. And use it every single time.

parts.name: Close
what_to_do: Ask: 'Will you commit to running this routine before every game?'
Affirm and close
parts.what_to_say: When I run this sequence—it's game time.

guides.age_band_notes: Some players will over-complicate their routine. Encourage: 'Simple and consistent is stronger than complex and sporadic.'

parts.name: Opening
what_to_do: Frame: 'Elite athletes don't leave mental state to chance. They run a deliberate protocol based on neurology and psychology.'
Play video (approx 120 sec); let the research on stimulus-response automaticity land
parts.what_to_say: Research on elite performers shows consistent pre-performance routines reduce variability across matches.

parts.name: Core concept
what_to_do: Debrief the protocol: Nervous system regulation → Competitive activation → Mental rehearsal → Intention setting
Key: 'Not vague. Specific. 'Today I bring relentless focus in transition,' not 'I want to try hard.'
Emphasise: 'When you run the same sequence, your nervous system learns: it's time to perform.'
Competitive angle: 'This is how elite athletes build consistency. You do the same preparation, you get the same readiness.'
parts.what_to_say: Same sequence, same state, same performance level.

parts.name: On-pitch drill
what_to_do: Drill: 'Protocol Design and Repetition'—each player builds their full protocol
Breathing sequence (specify: in for 4, hold for 2, out for 5), activation cue (specific word or phrase), visualisation focus (what decision, what execution), specific intention
Have them run it before a high-intensity game or drill
Repeat the protocol before a second activity; reinforce: 'Same sequence, same nervous system state.'
Observe automaticity improving
parts.what_to_say: Execute it identically every time. Your nervous system learns. Your performance becomes consistent.

parts.name: Close
what_to_do: Debrief: 'How did running the same protocol before two different activities affect your readiness?'
Affirm; optional: 'That's stimulus-response automaticity. Run it every time and it becomes your competitive anchor.'
parts.what_to_say: Same sequence, same state, same performance level.

guides.age_band_notes: This cohort will engage with the neuroscience framing ('stimulus-response automaticity') and will appreciate the precision of specific intention-setting over vague aspirations.

parts.name: Opening
what_to_do: Ask: 'You've been training your mind for eight weeks. What have you learned?'
Listen to 1–2 answers; affirm all
parts.what_to_say: You've done something most young players never do—you've trained your mind like you train your feet.

parts.name: Core concept
what_to_do: Recap the eight weeks in simple language: mental fitness, feelings, confidence, mistakes, focus, breathing, warm-up, and how to use it all
Key point: 'But here's the thing that matters most: none of this works if you don't use it in real games. Real moments. When it's hard.'
parts.what_to_say: Using it in real games, real moments, when it's hard—that's when the work shows up.

parts.name: On-pitch drill
what_to_do: Drill: 'Real-Game Application'—small-sided game with reflection breaks
During the game, pause and ask: 'Who just reset after a mistake? Who used their breathing? Who was confident?'
Affirm those moments specifically
After the game, ask: 'What's one thing you actually used from the last eight weeks?'
Have them pick one skill to make automatic
parts.what_to_say: Pick it. Use it. That's how a skill becomes automatic. Like passing. Like your position sense.

parts.name: Close
what_to_do: Ask: 'What's your one automatic skill?'
Affirm and close with the remember line
parts.what_to_say: I'm using this. Not just learning it.

guides.age_band_notes: At this age, learning happens through repeated application. Emphasise practice and use over 'understanding.'

parts.name: Opening
what_to_do: Play video (approx 115 sec); let the recap land
Pause after: 'Mental fitness is built in the moments between sessions—not in them.'
parts.what_to_say: The most important question now isn't what you've learned. It's: what have you actually used?

parts.name: Core concept
what_to_do: Reflect with them on the eight weeks: mental fitness awareness, emotional understanding, confidence, mistakes, focus, breathing, pre-performance routine, pressure reframe, team energy, resilience
Key: 'This isn't theory. This is a toolkit. And proof that it's working is when you use it in real moments.'
Emphasise: 'Mental fitness is built in the game where you reset instead of spiralling. The match where you ran your warm-up and felt the difference.'
parts.what_to_say: Mental fitness isn't built in sessions. It's built in the moments between sessions.

parts.name: On-pitch drill
what_to_do: Drill: 'Toolkit Application'—higher-intensity game with reflection pauses
During the game, notice and affirm when you see mental skills in action: 'I saw you reset there,' 'I saw you breathe before that moment,' 'I saw you help your teammate.'
After, ask: 'Which one skill are you committing to use every time?'
Have each player name one skill
parts.what_to_say: You've built the foundation. Now you build the habit.

parts.name: Close
what_to_do: Ask: 'What's the one mental skill you're committing to?'
Affirm and close
parts.what_to_say: I'm not just learning this—I'm using it.

guides.age_band_notes: This is the consolidation moment. Emphasis should be on behaviour change, not knowledge retention.

parts.name: Opening
what_to_do: Frame: 'Comprehension is not capability. Knowing about breathing isn't breathing under pressure. That's the gap we're closing.'
Play video (approx 120 sec); let the research on deliberate practice land
parts.what_to_say: Elite performers separate learning from doing. Gains occur not in passive consumption, but in struggle and iteration.

parts.name: Core concept
what_to_do: Reflect on eight weeks: meta-awareness, evidence-based confidence, attentional control, somatic regulation, pre-performance protocol, group cohesion, resilience activation
Key: 'Knowledge isn't capability. Where have you applied it under real pressure?'
Competitive frame: 'The critical question is not what you know. It's where you've applied it and how it's shifted your performance.'
parts.what_to_say: Your real Phase 1 outcome is behaviour change, not knowledge.

parts.name: On-pitch drill
what_to_do: Drill: 'Application Audit'—high-intensity game with structured debrief
Mid-game and post-game, ask each player: 'Which moment did you choose differently? Which skill did you deploy under pressure?'
Listen for specificity: 'I reset faster after that error,' not 'I used stuff.'
Affirm the precision of application
Have each player identify their anchor skill—the one they're mastering first
parts.what_to_say: You build mastery through repetition, not breadth. Choose one skill. Master that.

parts.name: Close
what_to_do: Debrief: 'Your anchor skill—the one you're committing to automatic mastery?'
Affirm; optional: 'Repetition creates automaticity. Automaticity frees your focus for the next layer. That's elite progression.'
parts.what_to_say: I apply, not just absorb. One skill, mastered.

guides.age_band_notes: This cohort responds well to the research on deliberate practice and will engage with the gap between knowing and doing. Frame mastery as the next level.

parts.name: Opening
what_to_do: Ask: 'Before a big game, what do you feel? Your heart fast? Your belly tight?'
Listen; affirm: 'That's normal. Everyone feels that.'
Introduce: 'And guess what? We can change what that feeling means.'
parts.what_to_say: Excitement and pressure feel exactly the same in your body. The only thing different is what you call it.

parts.name: Core concept
what_to_do: Say: 'When your heart races before a kick, your body is getting ready. That's not bad. That's your body saying: this matters. I'm switching on.'
Demo: 'Some athletes call it fear. Some call it excitement. Guess who plays better? The ones who call it excitement.'
Key: 'You can't turn off the feeling. But you can change what it means.'
parts.what_to_say: Feel the nerves. Then say: I am excited. Because you are. Your body is ready. Use it.

parts.name: On-pitch drill
what_to_do: Drill: 'Pressure Reframe'—small-sided game with high stakes introduced (e.g., 'Winner stays, loser sits')
Watch for signs of pressure (tight shoulders, rushed play)
When you see it, pause and say: 'You're excited. Feel that? Use that energy.'
Have them resume with that reframe
Observe improved performance after reframe
parts.what_to_say: Your nerves mean you care. Your energy means you're ready. That's not a problem. That's power.

parts.name: Close
what_to_do: Ask: 'Did anyone feel different when they said 'I am excited' instead of 'I am scared'?'
Affirm and close with the remember line
parts.what_to_say: I am excited. My body is ready.

guides.age_band_notes: Young players often feel shame about nerves. Reframe intensely: 'Nerves mean you care. Nerves mean you're ready. That's good.'

parts.name: Opening
what_to_do: Frame: 'What does pressure feel like to you?'
Play video (approx 115 sec); let the science land: 'The only difference is the story you tell yourself about what it means.'
Highlight: 'Researchers at Harvard found that athletes who said 'I am excited' performed measurably better.'
parts.what_to_say: Pressure means you care. Pressure means your body is ready. The athletes who learn to use it—instead of fight it—are the ones you want to watch.

parts.name: Core concept
what_to_do: Recap: pressure and excitement are neurologically almost identical
Key: 'The only difference is the label you put on it.'
Demo: 'Heart pounding, stomach tight, mind racing. Is that anxiety? Or is it readiness?'
Teach the reframe: instead of 'calm down,' say 'I am excited.'
parts.what_to_say: You can't turn off the pressure. But you can change what it means to you.

parts.name: On-pitch drill
what_to_do: Drill: 'High-Pressure Small-Sided Game'—introduce tournament stakes (winner advances, loser is out)
When players show pressure responses (tightness, rushed play), pause and say: 'Reframe. I am excited.'
Have them name the reframe, then resume
Observe decision-making and performance improve post-reframe
Repeat 2–3 times; reinforce the pattern
parts.what_to_say: Before your game, try this. Feel the nerves. Say: 'I am excited.' Let yourself feel it that way.

parts.name: Close
what_to_do: Ask: 'Did the reframe change how you played?'
Affirm and close with the remember line
parts.what_to_say: Pressure means I care. My body is ready. I use it.

guides.age_band_notes: This age band often ties pressure to shame. Reframe as science, not psychology: 'This is how your nervous system works. We're learning to use it.'

parts.name: Opening
what_to_do: Frame: 'Jamieson's Harvard research showed that reframing pressure as enhancing improves cardiovascular efficiency and working memory capacity.'
Play video (approx 120 sec); the neuroscience language will resonate
parts.what_to_say: This isn't positive thinking. It's neurobiological reality. When you reframe pressure as eustress, your vascular function improves.

parts.name: Core concept
what_to_do: Debrief the research: somatic markers of pressure and optimal arousal are indistinguishable
Key: 'Your appraisal determines the outcome.'
Teach the reframe: 'I am excited. My body is optimised.'
Competitive angle: 'Elite athletes don't eliminate pressure. They redirect it into performance-enhancing activation.'
parts.what_to_say: When you reframe pressure as eustress, your working memory capacity increases. Your decision-making sharpens.

parts.name: On-pitch drill
what_to_do: Drill: 'Eustress Activation'—high-intensity, high-stakes game (tournament-style)
When you observe pressure responses (hesitation, conservative play), intervene: 'Notice your state. Reframe as eustress. Notice the shift in your decision-making.'
Have them explicitly name the reframe and observe performance change
Repeat; reinforce the neurobiological shift in real time
parts.what_to_say: Your appraisal shifts your vascular tone, your hormone profile, your cognitive bandwidth. You're not managing anxiety. You're channelling eustress.

parts.name: Close
what_to_do: Debrief: 'How did your decision-making shift when you reframed pressure as eustress?'
Affirm; optional: 'That's a measurable difference in cognitive bandwidth. You've just experienced performance enhancement through appraisal.'
parts.what_to_say: I reframe pressure as eustress. My nervous system is optimised.

guides.age_band_notes: This cohort will engage with the research framing and the neurobiological mechanisms. Emphasise the measurable performance advantage of the reframe.

parts.name: Opening
what_to_do: Ask: 'Think about the best team you've been in. What made it good?'
Listen; affirm: 'That's what happens when people bring good energy.'
Introduce: 'And you can be that person. You can change how your whole team feels.'
parts.what_to_say: Your team's energy comes from what you bring to it. One player can change how the whole team feels.

parts.name: Core concept
what_to_do: Say: 'It starts with small things. What you say when someone makes a mistake. How you celebrate when someone does something good. Being the friend who lifts everyone up.'
Demo: respond positively to an imagined teammate mistake; celebrate a success enthusiastically
Key: 'When things get hard, choose to be the energy you want to see.'
parts.what_to_say: Today, pick one mate and tell them something real you noticed. Not just 'good game.' Something that matters.

parts.name: On-pitch drill
what_to_do: Drill: 'Team Energy Build'—small-sided game with emphasis on encouragement
During the game, model and affirm positive team energy: specific praise, helping teammates reset, celebrating small wins
After a mistake, watch for which players encourage their teammate vs. criticise
Affirm the encouragers: 'That's team energy.'
parts.what_to_say: When things get tough, and they will, choose to be the person who stays calm and helps everyone reset.

parts.name: Close
what_to_do: Ask: 'Who encouraged someone today?'
Affirm; close with the remember line
parts.what_to_say: I can be the energy my team needs.

guides.age_band_notes: Young players are concrete in their thinking. Give specific examples of 'good energy': encouraging a teammate after a mistake, celebrating a good pass, helping someone reset.

parts.name: Opening
what_to_do: Frame: 'Individual mental fitness makes you better. But the mental environment of your team shapes how everyone performs.'
Play video (approx 115 sec); let the concept of psychological safety land
parts.what_to_say: Your individual mental skills are amplified when your whole team practises them together.

parts.name: Core concept
what_to_do: Ask: 'What did the best team you've been in feel like?'
Help them identify: safe, honest, like you could take a risk
Say: 'That's psychological safety. And it starts with small things.'
Examples: what you say after a teammate makes a mistake, how you celebrate wins, whether you talk before games
parts.what_to_say: A team huddle, a shared cue word, a team reset signal—these aren't soft things. They're competitive advantages.

parts.name: On-pitch drill
what_to_do: Drill: 'Psychological Safety Build'—small-sided game with team structure
Introduce a team reset signal (e.g., all clap once) that anyone can call
Introduce a team cue word that builds confidence together
Observe: who uses it? Who responds? Affirm both
Reinforce: 'When your whole team is using these, your performance compounds.'
parts.what_to_say: Pick one person and give them a specific piece of encouragement. Not 'good game'—something real.

parts.name: Close
what_to_do: Ask: 'How did it feel when the whole team used the signal together?'
Affirm and close with the remember line
parts.what_to_say: I choose to be the energy I want to see.

guides.age_band_notes: This age band is beginning to understand group dynamics. Frame team mental skills as practical performance tools, not soft skills.

parts.name: Opening
what_to_do: Frame: 'Cohesion directly impacts performance. The mental environment you build shapes every player's nervous system response under pressure.'
Play video (approx 120 sec); let the research on high-performing teams land
parts.what_to_say: Elite-level performance is never individual. Cohesion is a measurable performance lever.

parts.name: Core concept
what_to_do: Debrief: 'A team's collective mindset about failure directly predicts performance recovery time.'
Key: 'The fastest teams aren't the ones with no mistakes. They're the ones with the best reset culture.'
Emphasise: 'What you say after a mistake, how you debrief losses, whether you recover together—these are structural performance variables.'
parts.what_to_say: You're not just a player in a team. You're a leader who shapes the mental environment every player performs in.

parts.name: On-pitch drill
what_to_do: Drill: 'Cohesion Architecture'—high-intensity game with team structure
Introduce team-wide resets (shared breathing, shared cue word, coded communication for pressure moments)
Observe: which players initiate? Which respond? Affirm both
During pauses, ask: 'How does your collective nervous system state shift when you use these rituals?'
Reinforce: 'You're not training individuals. You're architecting a team culture.'
parts.what_to_say: Model resilience. When pressure spikes, be the first to reset. That single action changes your team's nervous system response.

parts.name: Close
what_to_do: Debrief: 'How did your performance shift when the whole team was using the same reset protocols?'
Affirm; optional: 'That's what elite-level group cohesion looks like. You're not solo athletes. You're a system.'
parts.what_to_say: I build my team's mental environment. Cohesion is a skill.

guides.age_band_notes: This cohort will engage with the research on cohesion and will respond to the leadership framing. Emphasise the measurable performance advantage of collective protocols.

parts.name: Opening
what_to_do: Ask: 'Everyone has a bad game. Everyone gets dropped. Everyone has a week where nothing goes right.'
Wait for acknowledgement
Say: 'It's not about if it happens. It's about what you do next.'
parts.what_to_say: Bouncing back is a skill you can learn.

parts.name: Core concept
what_to_do: Say: 'When you have a setback, it's okay to feel sad or cross. Don't pretend it's fine when it's not. Feel it for a bit.'
Key: 'But then find one thing you can do something about. Just one. Not everything.'
Demo: 'Maybe you work harder in training. Maybe you ask your coach something. Maybe you talk to a mate. One thing. Do it.'
Emphasise: 'Moving is what makes you bounce back. You don't have to feel better first. You just move, and then you feel better.'
parts.what_to_say: You don't wait to feel better. You move. Then you feel better.

parts.name: On-pitch drill
what_to_do: Drill: 'Bounce-Back Scenario'—simulate a setback (lose a game, make a major error)
Ask players: 'One thing you can do about this?'
Have them name it and do it immediately (e.g., ask for feedback, work on a skill)
Observe and affirm the action
Repeat with a different scenario
parts.what_to_say: When you feel something strong, you don't sit with it. You notice it and you move.

parts.name: Close
what_to_do: Ask: 'What's your one thing you'd do after a setback?'
Affirm and close with the remember line
parts.what_to_say: I don't wait to feel better. I move. Then I feel better.

guides.safeguarding_note: If a player is experiencing a current setback (recent poor form, dropped from squad, family issue affecting sport), listen with care and normalise: 'This is hard. And you can move forward from here.' Consider mentioning support available (coach, parents, welfare officer).
guides.age_band_notes: Young players often believe setbacks are permanent. Emphasise: 'Setbacks are temporary. What you do next is what matters.'

parts.name: Opening
what_to_do: Frame: 'Resilience isn't about never feeling low. It's about what you do next.'
Play video (approx 115 sec); let the bounce-back process land
parts.what_to_say: The worst thing you can do after a setback is nothing. You don't have to feel better to start moving.

parts.name: Core concept
what_to_do: Introduce the process: Feel it (briefly, don't bury) → Find one thing you can control → Move on that one thing
Key: 'Movement creates momentum. Even small movement.'
Demo: 'After a bad game, I could spiral or I could ask my coach for specific feedback. That's moving.'
parts.what_to_say: Resilience is about what you do next. And you can start it today.

parts.name: On-pitch drill
what_to_do: Drill: 'Bounce-Back Under Pressure'—simulate setbacks during a game
Pause after a significant error and ask: 'Feel it. Now find one thing you can control. What is it?'
Have them name it and immediately do it (adjust technique, ask for help, reset focus)
Affirm the action and movement
Repeat 2–3 times
parts.what_to_say: Movement creates momentum. You're not waiting to feel better. You're choosing to move first.

parts.name: Close
what_to_do: Ask: 'When you moved after a setback, did it help?'
Affirm and close with the remember line
parts.what_to_say: I don't have to feel better to start moving. I just have to move.

guides.safeguarding_note: If a player is visibly struggling with a current setback, check in privately after the session. Affirm their resilience and connect them with appropriate support (coach, parents, welfare officer if needed).
guides.age_band_notes: This age band sometimes confuses resilience with emotional toughness. Clarify: 'Resilience is about moving forward, not about not feeling.'

parts.name: Opening
what_to_do: Frame: 'Elite athletes aren't protected from setbacks. They respond to them differently. The variable is time-to-recovery.'
Play video (approx 120 sec); let the research on elite recovery land
parts.what_to_say: What separates elite recovery from rumination is agency: seeing yourself as an actor, not a victim.

parts.name: Core concept
what_to_do: Debrief the behaviour markers: acknowledge the emotion → identify what's in your control → act immediately
Key: 'Neurologically, action precedes mood change in resilience. Movement doesn't wait for motivation. Movement creates momentum.'
Competitive angle: 'The fastest-recovering athletes move faster, not because they feel less. They act faster.'
parts.what_to_say: Elite recovery is action on what you control, not emotional avoidance. Movement precedes mood.

parts.name: On-pitch drill
what_to_do: Drill: 'Controlled Resilience Activation'—introduce significant setback during high-intensity game
When it occurs, intervene: 'Acknowledge. Identify the controllable variable. Act now.'
Have player name the emotion, name the variable, name the action
Watch the immediate momentum shift in their play
Repeat; reinforce: 'You're activating resilience through agency.'
parts.what_to_say: This week, identify one controllable variable you've been avoiding. Act on it. Now. Not when you feel better.

parts.name: Close
what_to_do: Debrief: 'How did your performance shift when you moved instead of ruminated?'
Affirm; optional: 'That's the measurable difference between resilience and avoidance. Be an actor.'
parts.what_to_say: I act on what I control. Action creates momentum. Momentum shifts state.

guides.safeguarding_note: If a player is experiencing a significant setback (serious injury, major loss, dropped from preferred team), check in privately. Affirm resilience and ensure they're connected with appropriate support (coaches, parents, welfare officer, counselling if available).
guides.age_band_notes: This cohort can handle the neuroscience framing and will respond to 'this is how elite athletes think.' Emphasise measurable recovery time and agency.

parts.name: Opening
what_to_do: Say: 'Twelve weeks. You've done something most young players never do.'
Play the video (approx 100 sec); let the pride land
parts.what_to_say: You've trained your mind. Not just your feet. Your mind.

parts.name: Core concept
what_to_do: Recap the twelve weeks: mental fitness, feelings, confidence, mistakes, focus, breathing, warm-up, pressure, team, setbacks
Key: 'None of this works if you don't use it. Using it in real games. Real moments. When it's hard.'
Emphasise: 'When you've practised something, it sticks. In big games. In tough moments. When it counts.'
parts.what_to_say: It's not something you've been told. It's something you've done.

parts.name: On-pitch drill
what_to_do: Drill: 'Phase 1 Celebration Game'—small-sided game that celebrates the twelve weeks
During the game, pause and ask: 'Who used something from the last twelve weeks? What was it?'
Affirm each response; make it visible and celebrated
After, ask: 'What's your strongest mental skill?'
parts.what_to_say: Now pick one thing—just one—and make it automatic. Use it every single time until it's as natural as passing.

parts.name: Close
what_to_do: Ask: 'What's one thing you'll keep using?'
Affirm and close with the remember line
parts.what_to_say: I've done the work. I'm ready.

guides.age_band_notes: Celebrate effort and engagement, not just outcomes. Young players respond well to visible, specific affirmation of their work.

parts.name: Opening
what_to_do: Frame: 'You've built a mental fitness foundation. Now make it automatic.'
Play the video (approx 115 sec); celebrate the toolkit
parts.what_to_say: Before you walk into today's showcase, take a moment. Think about who you were on week one. And who you are now.

parts.name: Core concept
what_to_do: Reflect on the toolkit: mental fitness awareness, emotional understanding, confidence triangle, mistake reset, focus control, box breathing, pre-performance routine, pressure reframe, team energy, bounce-back process
Key: 'Here's what happens with practised skills: they become automatic. Under pressure. In the big moments. When it counts.'
Emphasise: 'That's when Phase 1 starts paying off.'
parts.what_to_say: You've done the work. You know what you can do. You're ready.

parts.name: On-pitch drill
what_to_do: Drill: 'Phase 1 Application Game'—high-intensity game celebrating the twelve weeks
During the game, observe and affirm when you see mental skills in action
Pause and ask: 'What's one tool from the last twelve weeks that you used just then?'
Affirm specifically
After, ask: 'What's your strongest mental skill?'
parts.what_to_say: Just be yourself. The version of yourself that's been showing up for twelve weeks. That player is more than enough.

parts.name: Close
what_to_do: Ask: 'What's your commitment for Phase 2?'
Affirm and close
parts.what_to_say: I've done the work. I'm ready.

guides.age_band_notes: This is the consolidation moment. Affirmation should be specific and linked to observable behaviour, not generic praise.

parts.name: Opening
what_to_do: Frame: 'You've completed Phase 1. Twelve weeks of deliberate mental fitness training.'
Play the video (approx 120 sec); let the research on elite development land
parts.what_to_say: You're not entering Phase 2 as a novice. You're entering with a foundation that 95 percent of competitive young athletes don't have.

parts.name: Core concept
what_to_do: Reflect on twelve weeks: meta-awareness, evidence-based confidence, attentional control, somatic regulation, pre-performance protocol, group cohesion literacy, resilience activation, integration
Key: 'Comprehension is not capability. Where have you applied this under real pressure?'
Emphasise: 'That's your real Phase 1 outcome. Not knowledge. Behaviour change.'
Competitive frame: 'You've positioned yourself for elite-level development. What you do with this foundation next is up to you.'
parts.what_to_say: You've built meta-awareness, evidence-based confidence, and regulated nervous system response. These are your elite-development foundation.

parts.name: On-pitch drill
what_to_do: Drill: 'Phase 1 Integration Match'—high-intensity competitive match celebrating twelve weeks
Throughout, observe and note when you see mental skills deployed under pressure
Pause and affirm: 'I saw you reframe that pressure as eustress. I saw you use your team cohesion protocol. I saw you reset after that error.'
After, ask: 'Which skill did you lean on most under pressure today?'
parts.what_to_say: Under competition pressure, these protocols become automatic. That's where the edge comes from.

parts.name: Close
what_to_do: Debrief: 'You've built a foundation. Now Phase 2 is about deepening and applying it under increasing pressure.'
Ask: 'What's your anchor skill heading into Phase 2?'
Affirm and close
parts.what_to_say: Phase 1 foundation embedded. Ready for elite-level development.

guides.age_band_notes: This cohort will engage with the forward-looking narrative and elite-development framing. Emphasise continuity and deepening, not restart.
